title: Talking about my life - Boulder, Colorado 4/21

transcript.text: Okay, so yeah, Vidya.
So yeah, I remember when I was like one of the younger first kids there,
Orientating myself and stuff.
We had this like, it was like in the north part of town in this one house.
It was kind of like a big house, but not that big.
And it was like, I don't know how they fit all the kids in there.
Maybe it was only like a couple of grades.
And then maybe the bigger kids were on some other building or something.
But yeah, we were in the basement, bottom floor.
Yeah, wait, Mr. Brown was the teacher.
And so yeah, there was Jonathan McKeever.
And then there was this other kid who was a friend of mine.
I guess you could say it was one of my other best friends.
Michael.
No, no, Jesse Grimes.
Who was this like, I was one of the smallest kids, the shortest kid,
But he was like the only kid who was actually shorter than me.
So I thought that was cool.
But he was pudgy.
And he lived like two houses away from Jonathan McKeever.
Like on the old part of town near Pearl Street Mall.
And so they were best friends.
Because they were like neighbors and they knew each other.
And so I'd go and I'd spend the night and hang out with Jonathan most of the time.
And then we'd go over and visit Jesse Grimes.
And once I'd go and spend the night with him.
And once he'd spend the night with me,
And it was the first time we actually stayed up all night long.
We thought that was interesting.
And to stay up, we'd like give each other like back massages and stuff.
And we like made it.
You know, the sunrise came.
We were like, we stayed up all night.
Because, you know, kids get tired and they go to sleep.
But we had, but I think towards the end of one of those years,
Like we had problems.
I had problems with him.
Like he didn't like me for some reason.
And then I remember like Jonathan like got pissed off at him too.
And was like, yeah, he's doing the same thing with me that he does with you.
Or he doesn't like me.
And so they had kind of like, and I was like, yeah, he's your best friend.
He's like, no, he's not.
And then there was this other kid, Michael Bandak, who I hung out with a lot.
I'd spend the night with his house and stuff.
But he was kind of weird.
Like I'd spend the night and hang out with him.
And then all of a sudden he'd be hanging out with like neighbor kids.
And like I'd be in this room.
Like he'd like go off on errands with them and like not invite me.
And it was weird.
And then once, once his mom got like pissed off at him for doing that.
But he had like a bike.
And then he was, he's like, said he was like the best bike ever.
This BMX bike.
It was like, because it was rated five star for, because it had the best balance ever.
He was like one of these kids.
He liked to brag.
And like once I went skiing with him and he couldn't ski or he couldn't figure out how to.
It was at Hidden Valley Ski Resort.
Because when I was eight, I went skiing for the first time in Eldora.
And it was my aunt, my mother's brother's wife, who was like teaching us how to ski.
And she was really like insistent that we know how to sidestep first.
And then we skied and I went down the thing and I went down a bunch of times.
And I was like, this is awesome.
And then after that, we started skiing all the time.
Every weekend at Hidden Valley Ski Resort.
Instead of Eldora, I don't know why Eldora, but Hidden Valley.
Yeah, and once I went skiing with Jonathan McKeever.
And we were like, we had a ski instructor.
And we would like ski really fast and like tuck.
And he's like, you're not Bill Johnson.
Don't tuck all the time.
I mean, even in snow plowing, he'd be in like tuck position.
It was funny.
So, because Bill Johnson was the gold medalist for downhill that year.
This was in 84.
And yeah, but once we went skiing with Michael Bandek, my dad brought us up.
And he couldn't figure out how to ride up on the, I don't know what they're called.
The thing where you put the round thing between your legs and you have to stay standing up.
And it pulls you up.
But he thought you would sit on it.
But it has like string.
So he'd sit on it and then he'd fall down.
And I'd have to wait on the top.
And the lift up was like, yeah, your friend is still trying to figure out how to do it.
And after like a half hour, like a ridiculously long time, he finally figured out after falling
Down like a hundred times, figured out how to stand up on it.
And then we went skiing down, but he wasn't very good.
He kept falling.
And so he only did one run and he didn't want to ski anymore, but he wanted to do stunts
Because he wanted to be a stuntman when he grew up.
So we were behind on some like big pile of snow that the snow plow created and like falling
Down on it.
And like, he's teaching me how to like fall the right way and stuff, but we got lost to
Track of time and the ski resort closed.
And like my dad like went around the edge, was like, there you guys are.
I've been looking all over for you.
And he was all pissed off that we like lost a track of time and didn't, didn't meet him
Where we were supposed to or whatever.
But, um, but I remember the inside of that place at the bottom of the ski hill, there was
A, you'd have lunch and they had played.
I remember they played like eighties music all the time.
There's one song that I remember hearing what it was though.
But, uh, yeah.
And there was like, you'd have, I remember you'd take the bus up to near, near that, you
Know, that road, the top of the pass there and then ski down like the avalanche chute.
And then there was some more like trails and lifts you go up.
But my dad never let me, he said it was like, I wasn't old enough.
So I never actually rode that.
And then we stopped going there and we started, but then we owned like three houses on this
38 acres of land right at the base of Longs Peak near Fox Creek, called the Fox Creek.
And then when like, you know, we had this like trail that went down to the river and up on
The, and so I'd ride my mountain bike or my dirt bike, my BMX bike all over there, man.
It was fun to the top of the hill and around.
And once my brother and I rode our bikes all the way up to Ferncliff, the store, or to
The town, I think it was called Ferncliff, up a hill and then way down the hill to some
Town, the store called Ferncliff, where we actually adopted a cat who we named Fernie,
Who was a crazy cat.
And when we moved houses, we didn't bring the cat.
Like the Mexican maid tried to put him in a box and he jumped out of the box and ran off
And so we just left the cat at the house.
But, um, but, um, yeah, we, we rode that day like miles, it was like four miles, which
Is like a long time or four miles up and back or something.
It was like a long, it was right on our, to do on a BMX bikes for kids who were like seven
Years old.
Um, but we, uh, yeah, so we had like, you know, we, it was this like log cabin and it was a
Cool time being out there in the middle, in the mountains and stuff.
And once we, there was this, we, we had like the big, like summertime, like party, Buddhist
Party.
And so all the Buddhists came out there and, uh, they had this, like hundreds of Buddhist
People went out there and they camped out in their like, um, tents and stuff.
And they built, built this huge bonfire that was like 30 feet high up on top of the hill.
And I got drunk.
I was like eight years old or something like that.
And I got drunk on wine because there was always like copious amounts of alcohol of like
Wine because the leader, Shogun Chenpa Rinpoche was always wasted out of his mind on wine,
On red wine.
Like one time he came over to our house because there was just like really old white guy there
Who's staying with us for the summertime for in Boulder.
And then they'd go up and do the RMDC, but they'd have the things in Boulder.
And so he went over there to hang out with him and his servant boiled up wine.
I guess he liked to dream of warm wine and, but he wasn't there very long.
So he left.
So the servant just kind of left the wine, just turned off the heater.
And there was like wine with like frothing, you know, from being boiled, frothing and dripping
Down.
And I was like, that's gross.
Cause the guy, like the Rinpoche was, had to, couldn't go like more than five minutes
Without drinking wine.
It was like, and he'd like, and I remember like he'd do the talks like at the RMDC and
Stuff and I never actually talked to him personally, but I remember like looking at it and looking
Up at him and like seeing him and like, he was like, his eyes were all always like totally
Glazed over, like shiny and like almost like tearing from being drunk.
And I remember knowing that that was what it was like being drunk.
And he, he had this really high pitched voice like, but he was like slurring all the time.
He was like, and he spoke really slowly and he'd be like, he was like the drunken teacher.
It was really funny.
But because of that, like everybody thought that was cool to be like drunk all the time.
And my dad was a big time alcoholic, man.
The whole time I grew up, he quit cold Turkey and joined AA and was sober for like eight years
Or something.
He quit in like 1990 or 89 or something like that.
But my dad was always drunk drinking and stuff every day.
We'd go to the liquor store all the time and he'd get stocky, but like not him, not just
Him, but everybody.
So like, yeah, there was all this bottles of wine.
And so I got wasted on the wine and I threw a wine bottle at my mom at that.
I got pissed off at her and threw a wine bottle at my mom at that like bonfire party.
And then the next day, like remember Shay was there like shooting rifles.
He liked to shoot rifles.
And so we hung out with him a little bit and I think I shot one because he, he had these
Like guys surrounding him all the time, like dressed in military outfits in these khaki,
Short khaki pants, long pants.
And, you know, with these like hats on and like, they'd stand out front with these like
Rifles out front of his house.
It was like this really weird, like ceremonious type of thing.
And, uh, I thought that was kind of funny.
And yeah, that one time he came over, like the guy, he had a, he went around in a limo
And the guy like drove us around the block in his limo.
Cause we thought the limo was cool.
It was like an old limo.
But, uh, there was this other guy who stayed with us too named Ada Roshi, who was a Thai
Monk.
And he was like the leader of some monastery in like, uh, Massachusetts or something.
And when, and like their business on their land is they made, um, they made syrup.
So he'd, he'd come by every, every summer and like bring us all this syrup that I thought
Was really good.
And once I had a really long, like philosophical conversation with him, with my whole family
There, but I was like, he's telling us about all the different spiritual realms and like
Reincarnation, everything.
And I was like, so what realm are the dinosaurs in?
He's like, well, they're, what are they?
Are they animals?
I was like, yeah.
So he's like, they're in the animal realm.
So I was like, oh yeah, of course.
Uh, but he was really nice and he was really easy to talk to and stuff.
Um, and, um, yeah, but like the summer hanging out there in the summer over at RMDC was fun.
The little kids, they had like the, they had like King of the Mountain where you'd like
Jump on the top and then you have to push the kid off the top and then you got to be
King of the Mountain and then you'd like push the next guy off.
But I didn't really play that much.
I wasn't really all that physical of a, I wasn't, I was athletic, but I wasn't like
Aggressive.
Like when I was in, um, when I was in like school, we had our, when video, we had our
Buddhist soccer team.
Um, we were on the soccer and, you know, playing the soccer games with all the other
League and mini league, little league.
And, um, I only played one season there because I don't think I even once got to kick the ball
Because even though I was, I felt like, and I probably was the fastest runner.
I was a midfielder, but I was like the fastest runner and stuff, but I never, nobody ever
Passed the ball to me because, because you had to call out for the ball and I never called
Out for the ball because I felt like if I did call out for the ball, that would mean that,
That was like rude because the other kid was calling out for the ball at the same time as
Me and I didn't want to like piss him off by calling out for the ball instead.
So I just, what I did is I just ran out and just kind of assumed that the kid would see
Me and kick the ball to me, but they never did.
They always kicked the ball to the kid who called out for the ball.
So I kind of got bored with that and I didn't do that the next year.
And once we were playing, I remember we were playing soccer, um, at school and, uh, all
The kids like swarmed into the ball.
It was like this trying to kick the ball.
And I was just like the only kid.
I was just out on the outer edges of it, like a few feet away.
Cause I didn't want to deal with like attacking the ball.
I felt like that was a rude thing to do to like try and kick a ball.
Like I figured whoever was closest to the ball and got to the ball first had the right
To kick around the ball and you shouldn't like attack the ball and try to like take the
Ball from somebody else.
It just, so I was just kind of like standing away out and then the ball like just popped
Out to where I was.
So I kicked it and it went over the crowd of all the kids and it made a goal and everybody
Was like, Oh, fucking Kyle, I thought you made a goal.
It's badass.
I was like, I was like, yeah, but I was thinking like, whatever, dude, I just, I just got lucky.
And yeah, one time we played, um, we played, um, what was it?
Musical chairs and it was boys against girls.
And at the end it was me and against Cara Baker and I won.
And so all the kids were like, yeah, Kyle won.
It was like this huge celebration of the guys won.
Um, and, um, and another game that we played was, uh, that we played on school a lot.
And also in, uh, in summer camp over there, RMDC was capture the flag.
And that game was so much fun, man, running around, trying to pull the flag off of kids
And capturing the flag.
It was just fucking stalking each other.
It was so much fun.
Then we played that too at this other camp that I went to called something or other camp
Up in gold Hill.
Oops.
Out of time.
Out of time.
Out of time.
